research_focus: Decide whether the repo should add a smaller mobile-live Hibiki profile if the current `anquachdev/hbk-zero-3b-mlx-q4` path cannot get close to realtime on device, using upstream Kyutai mobile guidance and local benchmark evidence.
summary: Recommendation: yes, plan for a second mobile-live profile if the current q4 path stays far below realtime after substage profiling and one focused optimization pass. Upstream Kyutai smartphone guidance is tied to the smaller original Hibiki-M path on iPhone 16 Pro, while the current local repo is pinned to a 3B Hibiki-Zero q4 profile whose official upstream materials emphasize GPU use rather than iPhone deployment. The main tradeoff is that a mobile-live profile should improve throughput materially, but it may reduce speaker similarity and audio fidelity because the upstream mobile Hibiki-M variant uses 8 RVQ quantizers instead of 16, and it may also narrow language coverage if the mobile-live fallback is original Hibiki-M rather than Hibiki-Zero.

relevant_primary_sources.title: Kyutai Hibiki README
notes: States that Hibiki produces text and audio tokens at 12.5 Hz.
States that the MLX-Swift path can run on an iPhone and was tested on iPhone 16 Pro, but is experimental.
Lists two released sizes: Hibiki 2B with 16 RVQ per stream and Hibiki 1B with 8 RVQ per stream, described as ideal for on-device inference.

relevant_primary_sources.title: Hugging Face model card for kyutai/hibiki-1b-mlx-bf16
notes: Identifies the model as Hibiki-M for mobile.
Describes a 1.7B-parameter hierarchical Transformer producing tokens at 12.5 Hz.
Says the model is intended for real-time streaming translation settings.

relevant_primary_sources.title: High-Fidelity Simultaneous Speech-To-Speech Translation
notes: Reports that the smaller distilled Hibiki-M runs faster than real time on an iPhone 16 Pro.
States Hibiki-M is competitive with Seamless on short-form and long-form translation.
Explains that Hibiki-M has lower long-form speaker similarity because it models 8 quantizers instead of 16, giving half the audio bitrate.

relevant_primary_sources.title: kyutai-labs/moshi-swift README
notes: Frames the repo as an experimental iOS implementation for Moshi and Hibiki variants.
Says the main goal is experimentation on iOS devices and the included iOS app is only a proof of concept.
Uses `make run-1b`, which aligns with the smaller mobile-oriented profile rather than a larger profile.

relevant_primary_sources.title: Kyutai Hibiki-Zero README
notes: Describes Hibiki-Zero as a real-time multilingual speech translation model.
States that the released model is 3B and requires an NVIDIA GPU with 8 GB to 12 GB VRAM.
Does not present an official iPhone or MLX mobile deployment path.

relevant_primary_sources.title: Hugging Face model card for kyutai/hibiki-zero-3b-pytorch-bf16
notes: Describes Hibiki-Zero as a 3B-parameter model at 12.5 Hz and about 2.2 kbps audio bitrate.
Confirms current official release scope is multilingual X-to-English real-time translation.
Reinforces that the current official checkpoint family is large and GPU-oriented.

local_code_or_docs_to_check.reason: The app is currently hard-wired to `anquachdev/hbk-zero-3b-mlx-q4`, so any mobile-live plan requires profile-aware artifact selection.
Artifact preparation is pinned to one repo and one required Hibiki q4 weight file.
Documents that the current Swift runtime was built around a q4 Hibiki-Zero-style artifact with 32 total codebooks and 16 generated codebooks.
Provides the latest measured generated realtime factor and per-stage timings for the current path.
Confirms that live playback failure is mainly model starvation rather than only AVAudio configuration.
Defines the current interpretation of generated realtime factor and the model-flow timing report.
Shows the current playback sink choices that would need to become profile- or capability-aware.

implementation_implications: Keep the current `anquachdev/hbk-zero-3b-mlx-q4` path as a buffered or offline-capable profile unless profiling unexpectedly closes most of the 4.63x throughput gap.
Add explicit profile separation instead of pretending one model can serve both live and buffered use cases: for example `mobile-live` and `buffered-high-quality` or `buffered-multilingual`.
Make `ModelRuntimeManifest` and artifact preparation profile-aware so the app can switch model repo, tokenizer, bitrate assumptions, and supported-language messaging per profile.
Treat output-mode policy and model-profile policy as separate decisions: a fast model may still need buffering on slow hardware, and a slow model may still be valid for deferred playback.

[uncertain] If a mobile-live profile is added soon, the nearest official upstream precedent is original Hibiki-M 1B / 8-RVQ, not an officially released Hibiki-Zero mobile MLX profile. That likely means the mobile-live path would be a different model family with different language coverage, quality characteristics, and integration work.

realtime_budget_impact.current_local_state: The latest local benchmark generates 5.920 s of output audio in 27.463 s, for a generated realtime factor of 0.216x.
frame_budget_math: Hibiki and Mimi operate at about 12.5 Hz, so one frame is about 80 ms of audio.
Current measured average per generated frame is about 21.846 ms Mimi encode + 328.986 ms Hibiki step + 22.044 ms Mimi decode = 372.876 ms total.
Bare realtime requires the full pipeline to stay at or under about 80 ms per frame.
A practical live target around 1.25x requires about 64 ms per frame.
realtime_budget_impact.implication_for_current_q4_path: At 0.216x, the current path needs about 4.63x more throughput for bare realtime and about 5.79x for a practical live target. With encode and decode already near 44 ms combined, the remaining Hibiki budget would be about 36 ms for bare realtime or about 20 ms for a practical target, far below the current 328.986 ms Hibiki step average.
realtime_budget_impact.decision_signal: These numbers make the current q4 path look like a buffered or offline profile today, not a credible live profile.

measurement_plan: First split the current `Hibiki step` bucket into substages: main transformer, text sampling, Depformer loop, token extraction, MLX evaluation boundaries, and Swift materialization overhead.
Repeat the benchmark on the same fixture after one low-level cleanup pass to see whether the q4 path moves materially toward 1.0x or remains far below budget.
If a candidate mobile-live profile is available, run the same benchmark harness and device playback path on the same fixture and hardware.
Compare each profile on generated realtime factor, p50 and p95 stage times, memory pressure, startup time, visible-text cadence, underrun count, and final WAV artifact completeness.
Record profile-specific language coverage and quality observations, especially speaker similarity, naturalness, and translation adequacy.
expected_signal: If the q4 profile remains below about 0.5x after profiling and one focused optimization pass, it is too far from realtime to be the primary live profile.
If a smaller mobile-live profile reaches at least 1.0x on the target device and keeps playback underruns at zero after a bounded prebuffer, it is strong evidence to add it.

If the smaller profile reaches realtime but quality drops sharply in speaker similarity or naturalness, the app should expose it as a live experiment profile and keep the larger q4 path for buffered output.

[uncertain] If no official smaller Hibiki-Zero mobile checkpoint appears, the best early mobile-live candidate may be original Hibiki-M, which would improve speed but may trade away multilingual parity with the current Hibiki-Zero path.
recommended_next_step: Proceed with a dual-profile plan, but gate it on one short evidence pass: split `Hibiki step`, apply one profiler-led cleanup to the current q4 path, and if the resulting device benchmark is still clearly sub-realtime, add a separate mobile-live profile rather than continuing to market the q4 path as live-capable. In practice, keep the current q4 3B path as buffered or offline by default and treat the smaller profile as the live experiment path.
acceptance_criteria: The repo can describe at least two model intents clearly: a live-capable mobile profile and a buffered or offline higher-capability profile.
The current q4 profile is no longer assumed to be live by default when its measured generated realtime factor is below 1.0x.
A candidate mobile-live profile is evaluated on the same fixture and device with the same benchmark harness.
The mobile-live profile reaches at least 1.0x generated realtime factor on target hardware, with a practical goal of at least 1.25x.
Live playback with the chosen mobile-live profile finishes without repeated underruns after a bounded prebuffer.
Profile selection and UI copy make quality and language-coverage differences explicit.
risks: A second profile increases artifact-management complexity, app-surface complexity, and test-matrix size.
The upstream mobile precedent is for original Hibiki-M, not the exact current Hibiki-Zero q4 path, so profile split may introduce apples-to-oranges product comparisons.
The smaller mobile profile likely reduces speaker similarity and audio richness because the paper attributes lower long-form speaker similarity to 8 versus 16 quantizers and lower bitrate.
[uncertain] If the mobile-live profile uses original Hibiki-M, live mode may narrow source-language support from the current multilingual Hibiki-Zero scope to French-to-English only.
open_questions: How much of the current 328.986 ms `Hibiki step` is true model compute versus MLX-Swift boundary overhead, synchronization, or array materialization?
Can the current q4 path gain enough speed from substage fixes to change the product decision, or is the model-size and codebook budget itself the dominant blocker?
What exact language coverage does the product need for live mode versus buffered mode?
[uncertain] Is there an official or upcoming smaller Hibiki-Zero mobile checkpoint for MLX or iPhone, or would the live path need to adopt original Hibiki-M or an internally converted variant?
What minimum quality bar is acceptable for a live experiment profile in translation adequacy, naturalness, and voice similarity?
